result:
The sun had already begun its slow descent behind the jagged teeth of the Pyrenees when I first understood that silence is not the absence of sound but the presence of something heavier. I was twenty-four, alone in a stone shepherd’s hut somewhere above the Vall d’Aran, and the only noises were the wind worrying the slate roof and the faint metallic click of my cheap lighter refusing to spark. That click became a kind of meditation. Each failed attempt carved a small trench in the evening until the trench felt deeper than the valley below me.

I had come there to disappear for a while. Not in any dramatic sense—no one was chasing me, no heartbreak had driven me mad. I simply wanted to stand in a place where my own name would sound ridiculous if spoken aloud. There is a particular freedom in being unaccounted for. The mountains don’t require your résumé. They don’t ask what you do for money or whether you voted in the last election. They only ask whether you can carry water, build a fire, and accept that tomorrow might be colder than today.

For ten days I spoke to no one. I read nothing. I wrote nothing. I simply walked, ate, slept, and watched the light move across granite the way a child watches a candle. The silence did strange things to time. Minutes dilated into geological epochs. A single hawk riding a thermal became an event worth an entire afternoon’s contemplation. When I finally did speak—aloud, to myself, while trying to split kindling with a dull axe—the sound of my voice startled me so badly that I dropped the tool and laughed until I cried. The laughter echoed off the cirque and came back to me sounding like an entirely different person. Maybe it was.

That trip taught me something I have never managed to unlearn: most of what we call personality is just friction against other people. Strip the friction away and you discover how much of you is echo, how much is performance, how much is fear wearing the mask of opinion. In the mountains I met a quieter creature who needed far less to be content. He could sit for hours watching mist braid itself through pine trees and feel that this was a full day’s work. He did not need to be right. He did not need to be interesting. He only needed to be continuous with the world instead of constantly interrupting it.

I have spent the years since trying to smuggle that creature back into ordinary life. It is harder than it sounds. Cities are loud with forgotten things. Every screen, every notification, every polite conversation is a small vote for discontinuity. We fragment ourselves into bite-sized opinions, aesthetic choices, brand loyalties, and then wonder why we feel hollow. The shepherd’s-hut self does not disappear; it simply waits in the cellar of the mind like a patient animal, knowing we will eventually exhaust ourselves and come looking for water.

Sometimes I catch glimpses of him in other people. You can see it in the eyes of a baker who has been making the same sourdough for thirty years, or a fisherman whose hands move without thought between net and sea. There is a profound dignity in becoming so absorbed by a single thing that the self grows translucent. Not erased—translucent. You can still see the outline, but the light passes through.

I have never been very good at translucence. My mind is a magpie. It collects bright failures the way other people collect stamps. Half-finished novels, abandoned languages, relationships that died from too much analysis. For a long time I thought this made me interesting. Now I understand it mostly makes me tired. The older I get, the more I admire people who can stay with one thing long enough for it to stay with them. There is a kind of love in that fidelity that social media cannot simulate.

Last spring I spent three weeks in a small village in the Peloponnese helping an old man named Stavros rebuild the stone wall around his olive grove. Stavros spoke almost no English and I spoke even less Greek, so we worked mostly in silence. Each morning he would hand me a trowel and a level and then demonstrate, with theatrical slowness, exactly how he wanted the next course laid. His hands were astonishing—gnarled, liver-spotted, precise as a surgeon’s. He could run his thumb along a stone and tell you within a millimeter whether it would sit level. When I managed to place a stone correctly he would grunt once, which I came to understand was the highest possible praise.

At night we drank terrible retsina and he told me stories I could only half understand. Tales about his father who had fought in the civil war, about a olive tree that had been struck by lightning in 1973 but still bore fruit, about his wife Eleni who had died twelve years earlier but whose laugh he could still hear sometimes when the wind came from the south. I would nod in what I hoped were the right places. Mostly I just listened to the tone of his voice—the way grief and pride and ordinary exhaustion braided themselves together into something that sounded like acceptance.

One afternoon we unearthed a small terracotta figurine from the rubble. It was a woman, maybe four inches high, with arms raised in what might have been dance or prayer. The paint had worn away centuries ago but the gesture remained. Stavros held it in his palm like a baby bird. He said something I didn’t catch, then repeated it more slowly: “She was waiting.” He made me understand that the wall we were repairing had once been part of an ancient sanctuary. The figurine had probably been buried there as an offering two thousand years earlier. Now she had been freed by accident and would be reburied somewhere else, perhaps under the new wall. The thought pleased him. Continuity mattered more than novelty.

That night I dreamed I was the figurine. I had been waiting under stones for twenty centuries while the world argued above me about progress and identity and whether pineapple belongs on pizza. The argument sounded like distant bees. It did not concern me. My job was simply to keep the gesture intact until someone needed to remember how to pray with their whole body again.

I woke up laughing.

There is something fundamentally comic about being human once you stop taking the performance too seriously. We are the only creatures who can look at ourselves from the outside and still insist on wearing pants. We write symphonies and then use them in car commercials. We split the atom and then argue about whether the person who split it was nice. We invented both existential dread and dad jokes, often on the same Tuesday afternoon. The contradictions are not bugs in the system. They are the system.

I have started collecting these contradictions the way other people collect sea glass. Here are a few:

- The same species that produced Bach also produced reality television.
- We cry at weddings and then spend years avoiding the person we cried for.
- We spend billions developing telescopes powerful enough to see the edge of the observable universe and then use them primarily to take pictures of our own assholes from space.
- Every revolutionary movement eventually needs stationery.
- The more certain someone is about the meaning of life, the less likely they have understood the question.

The older I get, the more I believe that the only sane response to being alive is a kind of reverent bewilderment. Not nihilism—that’s just depression with better vocabulary. Not naïve optimism—that’s just denial with better marketing. Something in between. A willingness to be continuously surprised by how simultaneously beautiful and stupid everything is.

Last month I attended the funeral of a friend’s father. The man had been a physicist who spent his career studying quantum entanglement. In his eulogy my friend read from a letter his father had written him on his thirteenth birthday. It said, in part:

“Most people think the opposite of uncertainty is certainty. This is wrong. The opposite of uncertainty is wonder. Certainty is a temporary emotional state that should be treated like any other temporary emotional state—acknowledged, felt, and then gently shown the door. Wonder can be permanent if you let it.”

The letter was signed with a small doodle of Schrödinger’s cat wearing a party hat.

I have thought about that letter every day since. Wonder feels increasingly like a radical act. We live in an age that has outsourced its wonder to algorithms. Netflix tells us what to watch. Spotify tells us what to feel. Dating apps tell us who to love. The machines have become so good at predicting our desires that many of us have forgotten how to have desires that would surprise the machines. We have confused convenience with meaning and then wondered why life tastes like plastic.

But wonder cannot be predicted. It arrives sideways, usually when we have stopped looking for it. In the smell of rain on hot pavement. In the particular way an elderly woman in the supermarket touches every avocado before choosing one, as if listening for something the rest of us have forgotten how to hear. In the fact that octopuses apparently dream in color. In the way a stranger’s laugh on the subway can, for no reason at all, make you believe in God for seven entire seconds.

I have begun trying to protect my capacity for wonder the way previous generations protected their virtue. I delete apps. I walk instead of driving when I can. I spend one day a month deliberately incompetent—trying to cook something I’ve never cooked, attempting to draw something I’ve never drawn, speaking only in questions. The goal is not mastery. The goal is to remain porous.

The shepherd’s-hut self approves of this project, I think. He rarely speaks, but when he does it is usually to remind me that I am not the center of anything and that this is enormously good news. The universe, he points out, has been getting along quite well without my opinions for fourteen billion years. It will likely continue to do so. This realization is not depressing. It is the first day of summer vacation that lasts the rest of your life.

Sometimes I wonder what I will be like at eighty. I hope I will be the kind of old man who keeps a small notebook in which he writes down particularly good clouds. I hope I will have become boring to everyone except children and animals, who seem to recognize seriousness when it has been properly aged. I hope I will have finally learned how to sit still without feeling that I am wasting time. Mostly I hope I will still be capable of awe when the light hits a certain kind of granite at a certain time of evening, and that I will not have confused wisdom with resignation.

The wind has picked up outside my window as I write this. It is making the pine trees sound like the ocean, which is making me want to be near actual ocean. This is how desire works—one honest thing leads to another. I have been writing for hours now and my hands are beginning to cramp. The dog is looking at me with the particular expression she reserves for when she believes I have forgotten that the entire point of existence is walking.

She is probably right.

There are 2,487 words here if you count this sentence. That feels like enough. The rest of the afternoon belongs to silence and whatever ridiculous, magnificent thing might be waiting in it. The shepherd’s-hut self is already lacing his boots. I think I’ll go with him.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
